en.extract:
The campo miner (Geositta poeciloptera) is a Vulnerable species of bird in the subfamily Sclerurinae, the leaftossers and miners, of the ovenbird family Furnariidae. It is found in Bolivia and Brazil, and as a vagrant in Paraguay.


Taxonomy and systematics
The campo miner was originally placed alone in genus Geobates and has sometimes been treated there since, but genetic data place it firmly in Geositta. It is monotypic.


Description
The campo miner is a small member of its genus. It is 11 to 12 cm (4.3 to 4.7 in) long and weighs 17 to 19 g (0.60 to 0.67 oz). The sexes are alike. It has a dull rufescent brown face with a buff supercilium. It is dull grayish brown from its crown to its rump; the crown has vague brown spots and its rump and uppertail coverts have a rufescent tinge. Its tail is short; its feathers are rufous with a blackish brown band near the end. Its wing coverts are dark brownish with paler edges. Its axillaries ("wing pits") are cinnamon. Its flight feathers are blackish with a wide chestnut-rufous band that shows in flight. Its throat is whitish, its upper breast dull buff with brown flecks, its lower breast and belly whitish, and its flanks and undertail coverts pale rufous. Its iris is brown, its shortish bill is dark horn or blackish with a pale horn or pinkish gray base to the mandible, and its legs and feet are pale brown to pale gray or even dull pinkish.


Distribution and habitat
The campo miner is found in south-central Brazil, in an area roughly bounded by the states of Mato Grosso, Bahia, and Paraná, and in northeastern Santa Cruz Department in Bolivia. There is also at least one record in Paraguay. It inhabits campo grasslands and cerrado, open landscapes with at most scattered trees. It favors recently burned areas, especially for breeding. In elevation it ranges between 500 and 1,250 m (1,600 and 4,100 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The campo miner is non-migratory, but makes local movements among areas of suitable habitat.


Feeding
The campo miner forages singly or in pairs. It gleans food from the ground while hopping, not walking. Its diet is mostly arthropods; known items include Hymenoptera, Coleoptera, Mantidae, and spiders.


Breeding
The campo miner's breeding season has not been fully documented but includes at least September and October. The three known nests were in side chambers in armadillo burrows; the chambers were lined with dead grass and hair. The one clutch was of three eggs.


Vocalization
The campo miner's display flight song is a "high, level, hurried, rolling 'turruturrut---' " that lasts 15 to 20 seconds. Its flight call is "pit-pit".


Status
The IUCN originally assessed the campo miner as Near Threatened but since 2009 has rated it as Vulnerable. Its population size is not known and is believed to be rapidly decreasing. At least two-thirds of its original cerrado habitat has been converted to cropland, cattle ranching, and plantations of non-native trees. Ironically, fire suppression has reduced its breeding habitat in some areas. It occurs in some protected areas but is considered generally uncommon to rare. Brazilian authorities declared it Endangered in 2014.


References


External links
Image at ADW

The '''campo miner''' ('''''Geositta poeciloptera''''') is a [[Vulnerable species]] of [[bird]] in the subfamily Sclerurinae, the leaftossers and miners, of the ovenbird [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Furnariidae]].<ref name=IOC13.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/ovenbirds/ |title=Ovenbirds, woodcreepers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 13.1 |editor-last1=Gill |editor-first1= F. |editor-last2=Donsker|editor-first2=D.|editor-last3=Rasmussen |editor-first3=P. |date=January 2023 |access-date=27 April 2023 }}</ref><ref name=IUCN/> It is found in [[Bolivia]] and [[Brazil]], and as a [[Vagrancy (biology)|vagrant]] in [[Paraguay]].<ref name=SACCcountries>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, J. F. Pacheco, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. 28 March 2023. Species Lists of Birds for South American Countries and Territories. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCCountryLists.htm retrieved April 15, 2023</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The campo miner was originally placed alone in genus ''Geobates'' and has sometimes been treated there since, but genetic data place it firmly in ''Geositta''.<ref name=CAMI-BOW>Remsen, Jr., J. V. and C. J. Sharpe (2020). Campo Miner (''Geositta poeciloptera''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.cammin2.01 retrieved May 2, 2023</ref> It is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC13.1/>

==Description==

The campo miner is a small member of its genus. It is {{convert|11|to|12|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|17|to|19|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The sexes are alike. It has a dull [[rufescent]] brown face with a buff [[supercilium]]. It is dull grayish brown from its crown to its rump; the crown has vague brown spots and its rump and uppertail [[Covert feather|coverts]] have a rufescent tinge. Its tail is short; its feathers are rufous with a blackish brown band near the end. Its wing coverts are dark brownish with paler edges. Its [[Glossary of bird terms#axillaries|axillaries]] ("wing pits") are cinnamon. Its flight feathers are blackish with a wide chestnut-rufous band that shows in flight. Its throat is whitish, its upper breast dull buff with brown flecks, its lower breast and belly whitish, and its flanks and undertail coverts pale rufous. Its iris is brown, its shortish bill is dark horn or blackish with a pale horn or pinkish gray base to the [[mandible]], and its legs and feet are pale brown to pale gray or even dull pinkish.<ref name=CAMI-BOW/><ref name=vanPerlo>{{cite book | last =van Perlo | first = Ber| title =A Field Guide to the Birds of Brazil | publisher =Oxford University Press | date =2009 | location =New York | page =210 | isbn =978-0-19-530155-7 }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The campo miner is found in south-central Brazil, in an area roughly bounded by the states of [[Mato Grosso]], [[Bahia]], and [[Paraná (state)|Paraná]], and in northeastern [[Santa Cruz Department (Bolivia)|Santa Cruz Department]] in Bolivia. There is also at least one record in Paraguay.<ref name=CAMI-BOW/><ref name=SACCcountries/> It inhabits campo grasslands and ''[[cerrado]]'', open landscapes with at most scattered trees. It favors recently burned areas, especially for breeding. In elevation it ranges between {{convert|500|and|1250|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}}.<ref name=CAMI-BOW/><ref name=vanPerlo/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The campo miner is non-migratory, but makes local movements among areas of suitable habitat.<ref name=CAMI-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The campo miner forages singly or in pairs. It gleans food from the ground while hopping, not walking. Its diet is mostly [[arthropod]]s; known items include [[Hymenoptera]], [[Coleoptera]], [[Mantidae]], and spiders.<ref name=CAMI-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The campo miner's breeding season has not been fully documented but includes at least September and October. The three known nests were in side chambers in [[armadillo]] burrows; the chambers were lined with dead grass and hair. The one clutch was of three eggs.<ref name=CAMI-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Geositta-poeciloptera |species=campo miner}}
===Vocalization===

The campo miner's display flight song is a "high, level, hurried, rolling 'turruturrut---' " that lasts 15 to 20 seconds.<ref name=vanPerlo/> Its flight call is "pit-pit".<ref name=CAMI-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] originally assessed the campo miner as Near Threatened but since 2009 has rated it as Vulnerable. Its population size is not known and is believed to be rapidly decreasing. At least two-thirds of its original ''cerrado'' habitat has been converted to cropland, cattle ranching, and plantations of non-native trees. Ironically, fire suppression has reduced its breeding habitat in some areas.<ref name=IUCN/> It occurs in some protected areas but is considered generally uncommon to rare. Brazilian authorities declared it Endangered in 2014.<ref name=CAMI-BOW/>
